text: This evening, John chapter number 15, the portion of Scripture that is before us is often referenced because of a phrase that's quoted from verse number 5 at the end of the verse when Jesus said, for without me ye can do nothing. And certainly that statement of Scripture is worthy of our attention and worthy of preaching and messages that have focused in on those particular words and how important they are. This evening, however, I want to look at the greater context and look at these eight verses. So begin reading with me in John 15 in verse number 1. The Bible says this, I am the true vine. So Jesus is speaking and of course he's getting ready to go to the cross of Calvary. And in these chapters here in the midst of the book of John, Jesus is providing instruction that would be so important and so valuable for the disciples. And he says, I am the true vine. My father is the husbandman. Every branch in me that beareth not fruit, he taketh away. And every branch that beareth fruit, he purgeth it, that it may bring forth more fruit. Now ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you. Abide in me and I in you as the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, except it abide in the vine. Nor can ye except ye abide in me. Verse 5, I am the vine, ye are the branches. He that abideth in me and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit. For without me ye can do nothing. If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch and is withered. And men gather them and cast them into the fire and they are burned. If ye abide in me and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what you will and it shall be done unto you. Herein is my father glorified that ye bear much fruit. So shall ye be my disciples. As we look at this portion of scripture from verse number one, I've simply entitled the message this evening, the husbandman purgeth, the husbandman purgeth. And again, the reference in verse number five is quite familiar and one that most of us have heard perhaps many times in our Christian life. But it's interesting, as you examine the text of scripture, immediately we discover a truth that God wants his people to understand. Of course, as you look here in these verses of scripture, there are many truths that are of great importance. But I want to draw your attention to one particular truth that we see here in the text of scripture. One that we've at times overlooked and one that is so important for us to grasp and understand. According to verse number five, the Bible says, I am the vine. Notice this phrase, ye are the branches. And so who is he talking to? We know the word ye is plural. And so we know that he says in verse number two, every branch in me. And so he's talking about believers. They were not called Christians yet in John chapter number 15. We find that oftentimes they'd be referred to as disciples and you think about this idea of being a follower of Jesus Christ and those that have been saved and born again, followers of Christ. If we could look at it this evening when it talks about every branch in me and we begin to think and understand about the fact that we are in Christ and what a blessing that the Holy Spirit of God is within us and we have been sealed unto the day of redemption. And so we find this evening that the Lord Jesus Christ, he said in verse number one, I am the true vine. And then in verse number five, I am the vine, but he says, ye are the branches. So it's important for us to understand who we are, that we're the branches. But then I want you to notice specifically in verse number two, that there's a phrase that's repeated twice. And it's this phrase, every branch, every branch. And I want you to understand that every branch means every branch. There's no exception. It means every branch. And notice in verse number two, there's two particular categories. It says in verse number two, every branch in me, notice what it says, that beareth not fruit. He, who's the he, well, that's the husbandman, that's the father, he taketh away. The Bible continues in verse number two, and every branch, again, you see that phrase, every branch that beareth fruit, he, who's the he, well, that's the husbandman, that's the father, he purgeth it, that it may bring forth more fruit. The truth that we find as we compare verse two and verse number five, as we've looked at these statements and these words and understand them, that we would conclude this evening that I am a branch, and as a branch, I am to bear fruit. As a branch, I am to bear fruit. You are a branch if you're saved this evening. He is the vine, but ye are the branches, and we, as the branches, according to the truth of verse number two, and as you go through the text, it is God's plan and God's design that the branch produces, bears fruit. We understand that the branch cannot produce the fruit on its own, it bears the fruit, and he is the vine, and yet we find that in the context, the Bible would teach us that as a branch, I am to bear fruit, but as I'm bearing fruit, my father, your father, our father, the husbandman, he will purge so that I can bring forth more fruit. And that's why we deal with this subject, the husbandman purgeth. Notice with me, if you're taking notes this evening, just two thoughts. Number one, the process. The process. It's interesting that God would use throughout the scriptures, we understand the Lord Jesus Christ is a master teacher, oftentimes he would use parables, and oftentimes he would use analogies, things that were practical that people could understand, and they could take the spiritual lesson that Jesus was giving them, and they could understand it through the analogies that he would use. And here, obviously, dealing with husbandry and dealing with the branches and the vine and that whole process, and he uses that phrase in verse number two of purging. The word purgeth in verse number two, it carries with it the idea of to cleanse, but then we see that it has this idea, to prune trees and vines, notice this, from useless shoots. From useless shoots. And we're going to think about this idea of pruning trees and vines, and obviously the idea of the keeper of the vineyard, the keeper of the crop, they desire to have the greatest yield that they could possibly have. Therefore, the process is important that we would understand the process of purging or pruning, and we'd understand this evening that the lessons that we can learn from the agricultural implications in this text of scripture are so important to us as the people of God. Jesus used the illustration on purpose, the analogy on purpose, and it helps us to be able to relate to it. And so I want you to think this evening, when we think about the process, it begins by evaluating and identifying. Evaluating and identifying. The Lord knows everything about us. He knows everything about you, he knows everything about me, and he alone can identify what needs to be addressed. I think about the one that would be fulfilling this role, the individual at a vineyard or at some other property, and they would have this responsibility. They would first go out, listen, they don't just go out and start just chopping things. They first evaluate, and they identify, and it's a careful process. It's a process that is with purpose and intentional. When we find this evening that our husbandman, the Father, that he evaluates our lives, he knows us, he identifies in our lives, again, to prune trees and vines from useless shoots, and we begin to understand that process of evaluation and identification, and that he alone is able to do that. Then we find that the next step would be to eliminate competing branches. You know, there's oftentimes the reality in life that we can become divided. We can have allegiances and be divided, and sometimes you've even heard people say, I'm just one person, I can't be in all these different places at the same time, I can't do all of these things at the same time, and there are times in which I'll joke about different hats that I have that I'll put on, and someone let me know about a toilet that was clogged up, and I said I'd put my plumber hat on, and somebody let me know about an HVAC vent that needed to be replaced, and I'll put that, and I get it, and it's part of life, and it's what we do. But we begin to think about this idea of competing branches, because that's what the husbandman would do, he would evaluate and identify, and then eliminate competing branches. When I continue, or when I meditate upon that thought, it brought me directly to Hebrews chapter number 12. Look there, if you will, quickly this evening, and just begin to meditate on this process this week as I was looking over this portion of scripture, and thinking about this process of purging, of pruning. In Hebrews chapter number 12. We are familiar again with this text of Scripture and in verse number one The Bible says wherefore seeing we also are compassed about What's so great a cloud of witnesses? Notice what the Bible says. He says every branch that beareth fruit He purges it you understand if you begin to think about this process and again I'm not an expert at this but there will be some competing branches That will take away from the yield of the particular Tree that the husbandman has to eliminate And and we begin to think about this and listen we can think about this in a negative sense it's not negative It's actually positive. We ought to welcome the process in our life and we'll deal with that in just a few moments But we see here again this phrase every branch So I wonder this evening have we experienced the process have we been able to identify Times in our lives when the Lord the the God of glory the husbandman the father is purging When he's purging Have we experienced the process we understand in the beginning of the text? They're gonna be burned up and We see here that as we look at this text of scripture and we consider the process and again I'm hoping that each of us could be in that category where we're one of the branches every branch that beareth fruit He purges it why that it may bring forth more fruit in that process of purging I want you to understand this evening that the husband men the vine dresser the one that would do this work the one that they could relate to that they would understand the Analogy that's being used that they had tools that they would use in fact oftentimes with their tools They would want to make sure that those tools were very sharp Not only would they want to make sure that those tools were sharp but often as I did some studying this week I discovered that as they would prune as they would purge as they would go through that process They would clean their tools so that there were not diseases that were spread from one tree or one vine to another You're gonna think about that process again something that we today have to do some research about to learn some things about but many Here during this era of time as Jesus would use this they were familiar With these things and I want you to see this evening that the tool that is used is the perfect preserved powerful Word of God I'm thankful this evening The Bible says that the Word of God is quick and powerful and sharper than any two-edged sword and in Hebrews chapter number four and begin to think about the way that God uses his word in this process of purging and again to evaluate and identify and God will take and he'll he'll use Scripture and through the preaching of the Word of God Through the man of God through the time in which you read your Bible and your personal devotions and God will use his word and he's seeking to use the the powerful sharp Word of God to Eliminate those competing branches. Listen God Established the home not only has God established the home, but God has given Instruction for the home and here in Ephesians 5 you begin to see some of that instruction beginning in verse 22 But I want you to see how that the Lord uses and goes back to this truth that we're speaking of with regard to the The use of the tool of the Word of God in verse number 25 He says husbands love your wives Even as Christ also loved the church and gave himself for it that he might sanctify and cleanse it notice what it says with the washing of water by the Word And I'm thankful to have the Word of God and I'm thankful for the the truth of of the the power of God's Word And the way in which God's Word Works in our lives it's amazing to me that you can read the Word of God and God can take that word and he can use it to Comfort us and he can use it to Convict us and he can use it to change us and oftentimes He'll do all three of those things at the very same time and sometimes even from the very same passage And it's amazing how God uses his Word the Bible tells us in Psalm 19 You don't need to turn there but we read about the word of the the Word of God that the law of the Lord is Perfect and we read all of these wonderful things that the psalmist mentions about the word in Psalm 19 But then he says this in verse 10 more to be desired are they than gold? I'm thankful for the process. Now understand that the process has a purpose. So we see number one, the process, but we see number two, the purpose. What is the purpose? Understand this evening that purging is not punishment. Purging is not punishment. If you're not careful, you can get that confused. You can get the purging and the chastisement confused. See, the Bible does teach us that whom the Lord loveth, he chasteneth. And there's the truth of chastisement. We ought to appreciate chastisement. Many are sick, and many have slept. They've died the chastening of the Lord. But when we think about purging, as we look here in this idea, in this principle that God gives us in John 15, purging is not punishment. Chastening is not punishment. Purging is to maintain health and productivity. That's the purpose, to maintain health and productivity. Listen, if you allow, if you go to some of these orchards, if you go to some of these vineyards and they're not maintained, if they're not kept, they don't produce like they could. They don't have the health that they should. But when you find someone that cares enough about the orchard, you find someone that cares enough about the vineyard, you have someone that has enough knowledge, and they go through the process, they're purging, they're seeking to eliminate those competing branches, why? For the health and for the productivity. And so we begin to understand this, and we'd understand this evening that strategic pruning actually encourages growth and increases yield. I remember the very first time, years ago, I ever cut back our crepe myrtles. Some of you guys came to church and said, pastor's crazy, he doesn't know what he's doing. I don't know what I'm doing when it comes to that stuff. Now that's pretty simple for us to understand. You separate a branch from the vine and it dies. But I want you to understand this evening that though he is the husbandman and though he knows exactly what needs to take place, he evaluates and he identifies and he uses his word. The Holy Spirit is active in using the powerful word of God and he desires to eliminate those competing branches. I want you to understand something vital this evening. He doesn't force it. He doesn't force it. He made us as human beings with the ability to obey and disobey. He's given to us a free will. He's given to us a free will. He can take and he can give us the scriptures. And so that reveals to me, and it should reveal to each of us, that though God is working and though He's using His Word and though the Spirit is working, we can quench and grieve the Spirit. No, we ought not. We ought to have that perspective that it's best for us to let Him have His way with us. He knows what's best. It's so that the one that's producing fruit can bring forth more fruit and eventually bring forth much fruit. Again, it's not about anything that we've done except simply abiding in Him and simply submitting to Him and simply yielding to Him. And those eliminating, those branches, those competing branches, if they're not eliminated, they start taking the nutrients that are designed for those branches that would bring forth the fruit and all of a sudden the yield decreases. As you evaluate your own life, as I evaluate my own life, do you see the husbandman purging? Have you experienced the purging, that process? Has God used His Word in your life through, whether it's the public preaching of the Word of God in a church service, or whether it's your own personal devotions as you've read the Bible, has God taken that tool, like a surgeon would take a scalpel and just take that tool, that sharp, that Word of God that is sharper than a two-edged sword, and used that Word in your life to just kind of purge, to prune? Why? For His glory and for our good, so that we might bring forth more fruit. Let's pray. Father, this evening we thank You for loving us. We thank You for the Word of God and for the truth that's contained therein. God, I'm thankful for this process that we see given to us in John 15. God, I'm thankful for the work that You've done in my life, and I think about the truth of Hebrews 12 when we think about those competing branches and we think about that weight which doth so easily beset us. You told us there in Hebrews 12 that we're to lay aside every weight. Lord, I pray that You would help us as we get into Your Word, that Your Word would get into us, and that God, as only You can, that You would evaluate and that You would identify and that You would eliminate those competing branches, that You would help us, Lord, to abide in You so that we could produce more fruit, so that we could bring forth much fruit and glorify our Father. We understand this evening that that's really the only purpose of our lives is to bring honor and glory to Thy name, and God, I pray that You'd help us to be able to examine our own lives. Lord, in just a few moments we'll observe the Lord's Supper, but even as we take these moments to observe and to consider what we've heard and to evaluate our lives, God, help us to be able to identify, is there any purging that's taken place in my life? How is God working? What is God doing? How is He purging me so that I could bring forth more fruit? Lord, I pray that You'd have Your will and way in the moments of invitation, and we'll thank You for it. In Christ's name we pray.

verse when Jesus said, for without me ye can do nothing.

and messages that have focused in on those particular words and how important they are.
This evening, however, I want to look at the greater context and look at these eight
verses.
So begin reading with me in John 15 in verse number 1.

that would be so important and so valuable for the disciples.
And he says, I am the true vine.
My father is the husbandman.
Every branch in me that beareth not fruit, he taketh away.

Now ye are clean through the word which I have spoken unto you.
Abide in me and I in you as the branch cannot bear fruit of itself, except it abide in the
vine.

Verse 5, I am the vine, ye are the branches.
He that abideth in me and I in him, the same bringeth forth much fruit.
For without me ye can do nothing.
If a man abide not in me, he is cast forth as a branch and is withered.

If ye abide in me and my words abide in you, ye shall ask what you will and it shall be
done unto you.
Herein is my father glorified that ye bear much fruit.
So shall ye be my disciples.
As we look at this portion of scripture from verse number one, I've simply entitled the
message this evening, the husbandman purgeth, the husbandman purgeth.
And again, the reference in verse number five is quite familiar and one that most of

that God wants his people to understand.

According to verse number five, the Bible says, I am the vine.
